blocks.text: When Samuel became old, he made his sons judges over Israel.

1 : , lit., “custom” or “judgment.” The king to whom the people look for justice will follow the typical ancient Near Eastern practices of taxation and conscription. The items in this list appear to be based on Solomon’s reign (cf. 1 Kings 4.7; 5.13).
The name of his firstborn son was Joel, and the name of his second, Abijah; they were judges in Beersheba.
2 : ‐ , the southernmost city in Judah and far outside Samuel’s jurisdiction in 7.15–17. It became an administrative center during the Judean monarchy.
Yet his sons did not follow in his ways, but turned aside after gain; they took bribes and perverted justice.

3 : Samuel’s sons, like Eli’s, are corrupt; Deut 16.19 denounces taking bribes. The expectation that their sons would succeed them is contrary to the other judges but in line with kings. Their unfitness to rule impels the elders to ask for a king.
Then all the elders of Israel gathered together and came to Samuel at Ramah,
4 : The were the leading and usually senior citizens.
and said to him, “You are old and your sons do not follow in your ways; appoint for us, then, a king to govern us, like other nations.”
5 : The desire to is opposed to the ’s desire that Israel be his special people.
But the thing displeased Samuel when they said, “Give us a king to govern us.” Samuel prayed to the Lord,
and the Lord said to Samuel, “Listen to the voice of the people in all that they say to you; for they have not rejected you, but they have rejected me from being king over them.
6–7 : Samuel and the are displeased by the request and consider it a rejection of the . So is kingship regarded as inherently evil? Or does the request demonstrate a lack of faith in the ?
Just as they have done to me, from the day I brought them up out of Egypt to this day, forsaking me and serving other gods, so also they are doing to you.
8 : The review of Israel’s history is in typical Deuteronomistic style (cf. 2 Kings 21.15).

Now then, listen to their voice; only—you shall solemnly warn them, and show them the ways of the king who shall reign over them.” The kingdom of Saul according to the book of Samuel. The dashed line shows the approximate outer boundary of his kingdom.
So Samuel reported all the words of the Lord to the people who were asking him for a king.
10 : is a play on the name “Saul”; see 1.17n.
He said, “These will be the ways of the king who will reign over you: he will take your sons and appoint them to his chariots and to be his horsemen, and to run before his chariots;

and he will appoint for himself commanders of thousands and commanders of fifties, and some to plow his ground and to reap his harvest, and to make his implements of war and the equipment of his chariots.
He will take your daughters to be perfumers and cooks and bakers.
He will take the best of your fields and vineyards and olive orchards and give them to his courtiers.
He will take one‐tenth of your grain and of your vineyards and give it to his officers and his courtiers.
He will take your male and female slaves, and the best of your cattle and donkeys, and put them to his work.
He will take one‐tenth of your flocks, and you shall be his slaves.
And in that day you will cry out because of your king, whom you have chosen for yourselves; but the Lord will not answer you in that day.”
18 : This verse is in characteristic Deuteronomistic language and resembles that of the framework of Judges (see, e.g., 3.9,15), in which the people constantly for relief from their oppressors.
But the people refused to listen to the voice of Samuel; they said, “No! but we are determined to have a king over us,
19 : Samuel listens to God, while the people
so that we also may be like other nations, and that our king may govern us and go out before us and fight our battles.”
20 : is elsewhere translated “judge.” is an idiom for military leadership. The people trust in a king instead of God for protection from their enemies.
When Samuel had heard all the words of the people, he repeated them in the ears of the Lord.
The Lord said to Samuel, “Listen to their voice and set a king over them.” Samuel then said to the people of Israel, “Each of you return home.”

Chs 8–12: The beginning of kingship in Israel. These chapters include five distinct episodes: the people’s request for a king and Samuel’s response (ch 8); Saul’s search for his father’s asses and his anointing (9.1–10.16); the designation of Saul by lot (10.17–27a); Saul’s victory over the Ammonites (10.27b–11.15); and Samuel’s farewell address (ch 12). The stories in 9.1–10.16 and 10.27b–11.15 are generally recognized as older and neutral or positive in their attitude toward monarchy, while the other three episodes, widely recognized as Deuteronomistic, view kingship with suspicion and depict the people’s request as a sin. In the end, God allows a king but also warns the people against letting their king lead them astray.
8.1–22: The people request a king.
22 : The will permit Israel to have a king even though he does not approve of their request. is editorial and sets the stage for Samuel to call another assembly in 10.17.
